title: Today's Tech Brief: Firefox 151 helps you edit PDFs – and switch OSes
summary: Firefox 151's PDF editing and OS switching features, plus Sam Altman on OpenAI's top token user.

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your tech brief. Let's dive into some interesting developments. First up, Firefox 151 is out, and it's got a couple of features that caught my eye. You can now edit PDFs directly in the browser, which is super handy. But what's even more intriguing is that Firefox 151 allows you to export your profile on Windows and then restore it on Linux, complete with extensions and themes. That's a big deal for those who switch operating systems frequently.

This new feature in Firefox 151 makes it easier for users to switch between different operating systems without losing their browser setup. It's a move that could make Firefox more appealing to users who work in multi-OS environments. I mean, who hasn't struggled with getting their browser settings just right on a new machine? It's like trying to find the perfect coffee mug – it's all about the little things.

Also making headlines is Sam Altman, CEO of OpenAI, discussing the company's top token user. While details are a bit scarce, it's clear that OpenAI is making waves in the AI space, and Altman's insights are always worth listening to. The world of AI tokens is complex, but essentially, it revolves around how AI models are being used and valued. I'm curious to see how this develops – it's like trying to predict the next big plot twist in your favorite show.

My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.

sources.title: Firefox 151 helps you edit PDFs – and switch OSes
